question: What kills Indians?

theme.label: Health and survival
readerQuestions: What is the survival or health outcome?
How much has it changed over time?
Is the measure about death, illness, or healthy years?
What does the national average hide?

plannedCharts.title: What kills the most Indians, 2023
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME Global Burden of Disease 2023 · estimated deaths, all ages, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The direct answer to the page question, ranked. Heart disease dwarfs everything: at about 3.1 million deaths in 2023 it kills more Indians than the next two causes combined.

plannedCharts.title: What kills babies (under 5)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · children under five, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: For the youngest, death looks nothing like the all-ages chart. The biggest killers are the complications of birth itself, prematurity, asphyxia, infection, not chronic disease.

plannedCharts.title: What kills children (5 to 14)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 5-14, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: School-age children are mostly past the dangers of birth, so accidents move to the front: drowning is the single biggest killer, ahead of cancers and road crashes.

plannedCharts.title: What kills young adults (15 to 49)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 15-49, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This is the chart the all-ages ranking hides. Among working-age Indians, road injuries and suicide are the third and fourth biggest killers, each around 1.5 lakh deaths, the tragedy of dying young.

plannedCharts.title: What kills the middle-aged (50 to 69)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 50-69, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: By middle age the non-communicable diseases take over completely. Cardiovascular disease alone kills over 1.2 million people aged 50-69, with cancer and chronic respiratory disease next.

plannedCharts.title: What kills the elderly (70 and older)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 70+, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: In old age, cardiovascular disease is overwhelming: nearly 1.6 million deaths among the over-70s in 2023, more than the next three causes combined, with chronic respiratory disease a distant second.

plannedCharts.title: How men and women die differently
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS Cause of Death 2022-24 (Registrar General) · share of all male vs female deaths
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The biggest sex difference is injuries: 12.6% of male deaths versus 7.5% of female deaths, men die far more from accidents, suicide and violence. Women carry more 'ill-defined' deaths, a sign their causes go less often recorded.

plannedCharts.title: The rise of heart disease, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why the heart sits at the top, and how fast it got there. CVD deaths roughly tripled from under a million in 1980 to about 3.1 million in 2023 as the population grew, aged, and urbanised.

plannedCharts.title: The four non-communicable killers
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · annual deaths, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The non-communicable surge, broken into its four engines. Together these are the bulk of Indian deaths today, and all four are rising.

plannedCharts.title: What India's own death survey finds, 2022-24
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS Cause of Death 2022-2024 (Registrar General) · nationally representative, verbal-autopsy
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's own nationally representative cause-of-death data, the strongest check on the modelled GBD estimates. It agrees on the headline, cardiovascular disease leads at 32.1% of all deaths, and unlike hospital records it counts the rural and at-home deaths most Indians actually have.

plannedCharts.title: The COVID scar, and the recovery
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: What the pandemic did to the death toll, and the crucial point the old data missed: it then receded. COVID deaths spiked in 2020-21 and fell back sharply by 2023.

plannedCharts.title: India's own death rate: spike, then recovery
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS 2023 (Registrar General) · deaths per 1,000 population
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Are Indians dying at a lower rate? India's own Sample Registration System says yes, with a clear COVID interruption: the crude death rate jumped to 7.5 in 2021, then fell back to 6.4 by 2023.

plannedCharts.title: The retreat of infectious disease
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · annual deaths, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The other half of the story: as non-communicable deaths climb, infectious killers have fallen steeply. This is the epidemiological transition in one chart.

plannedCharts.title: Death by injury and suicide
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · annual deaths, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Injuries kill working-age Indians in their prime. Suicide and road crashes are the two largest, each around 200,000-250,000 deaths a year.

plannedCharts.title: Maternal mortality: the steepest fall
plannedCharts.subtitle: WHO/UN MMEIG · maternal deaths per 100,000 live births
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single biggest public-health win on this page. Maternal deaths fell from 658 to about 80 per 100,000 live births, a nearly 90% decline.

plannedCharts.title: Fewer babies dying
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS 2023 (Registrar General) · infant deaths per 1,000 live births
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Child survival is the engine behind India's falling overall death rate. The infant mortality rate fell to 25 per 1,000 live births in 2023.

plannedCharts.title: Total deaths each year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The denominator behind everything else. Total annual deaths have risen even as the death rate has fallen, because the population is larger and older.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
